items.summary: Marvels, wonders, and monstrosities: something between a medieval bestiary and a scientific treatise on birth defects.

items.title: “Wretches, Speak Evil of Me”: Goethe and Schiller’s Xenions (1896 edition)
items.summary: One of the most elaborate works of literary insult ever written.

items.summary: Moneymaking caricatures by a teenage Monet, before he turned to Impressionism.

items.title: Fear and Fragility: The Glass Delusion and Its History
items.summary: In early modern Europe, around the time when lenses began to bring the world (and heavens) into newfound focus, patients started appearing in medical records with a particular ailment: a firm belief that they were made of glass. Tamara Sanderson investigates the source and manifestation of this delusion, and finds a psychological idiom that once carried the weight of what could otherwise not be said.

items.title: Calicornication: Postcards of Giant Produce (1909)
items.summary: "Tall-tale" or "exaggeration" postcards illustrating the bounties of California.

items.title: Charlotte Perkins Gilman’s Herland (1915)
items.summary: A utopian novel where men are no longer necessary.

items.title: Wayang Kulit: Raden Soelardi’s Illustrations of Javanese Puppets (1919)
items.summary: Illustrations of Javanese *wayang kulit* puppets with vivid colour and gilding.

items.title: The Blinkered Flâneur: Walking with Franz Hessel in 1920s Berlin
items.summary: Does the flâneur , that curiously modern figure who wanders metropolitan streets, have a political consciousness? For Franz Hessel — author of Spazieren in Berlin , “a memorization while strolling” that Walter Benjamin called “thoroughly epic” — the answer seemed to be no. Paul Sullivan explores Hessel’s perambulations through Berlin and the achievements and limitations of his vision.

items.title: Unbridled Passions: The Eight Horses of King Mu, Son of Heaven (ca. 1300)
items.summary: Unruly steeds from King Mu’s mythology, cavorting across a silk scroll.

items.title: Pigeons Transmogrified: Hannah Glasse’s Art of Cookery (1751 edition)
items.summary: A bestselling 18th-century cookbook, containing the first recipe for curry in English.

items.title: Snail Homes, Bog Bodies, and Mechanical Flies: Robert Testard’s Illustrations for Les secretz de l’histoire naturelle (ca. 1485)
items.summary: A wondrous illuminated manuscript, which gathers and illustrates the marvels of the world and beyond.

items.title: Sekka Zusetsu: A Book of Snowflakes (1832)
items.summary: Observations of “snow flowers” made by microscope in Edo-era Japan.

items.title: Typing for Love or Money: The Hidden Women’s Labor behind Modern Literary Masterpieces
items.summary: Taking dictation, revising manuscripts, typing copies, literary amanuenses often labour for little compensation and even less recognition. Christine Jacobson explores the neglected efforts of women like Theodora Bosanquet, Véra Nabokov, and Valerie Eliot, who — through their work as typists, editors, and champions — had a profound impact on modern literature.

items.title: Sara Weiss’ Journeys to the Planet Mars (1903)
items.summary: A forgotten bridge between Spiritualism and UFO encounters.

items.title: Autobiography of a “Jeep” (1943)
items.summary: A WWII propaganda film narrated as an "auto"-biography

items.title: Cybernetic Attention: All Watched over by Machines We Learned to Watch
items.summary: Before the attention economy consumed our lives, “pursuit tests” devised by the US military coupled man to machine with the aim of assessing focus under pressure. D. Graham Burnett explores these devices for evaluating aviators, finding a pre-history of the laboratory research that has relentlessly worked to slice and dice the attentional powers of human beings.

items.title: Flights of Fancy: The Nine Birds of Jacques de Fornazeris (1594)
items.summary: Etchings of birds in a somewhat theatrical style.

items.title: A Prophet of the Weather: Lantern Slides by Clement Lindley Wragge (ca. 1900–22)
items.summary: Slides from twenty years of lecturing about the workings of the universe and the fate of the soul.

items.summary: The memoirs of an aristocratic man revolutionised into an anarchist communist.

items.summary: In mid-19th century Italy, two eccentric aristocrats set forth on parallel projects: constructing ostentatious castles in a Moorish Revival style. Iván Moure Pazos tours the psychedelic chambers of Rochetta Mattei, optimised for electrohomeopathic healing, and Castello di Sammezzano, an immersive, orientalist fever dream.

items.summary: Illustrations of Abū Zayd and his adventures in double meaning.

items.title: Art in Art: Cabinets of Curiosity and the Rise of the Gallery Painting
items.summary: In the 17th century, emanating from Antwerp, a new genre of artwork came on the scene: paintings of paintings, works populated by a lush array of meta-images. From its origins in picturing private curiosity cabinets to its later use in documenting increasingly public collections, Thea Applebaum Licht charts the course of this alluring aesthetic tradition.

items.title: Roma Lister, Aradia, and the Speculative Origins of a Witchcraft Revival
items.summary: In 1899, Charles Godfrey Leland published Aradia , “the gospel of the witches”, containing a goddess-orientated creation and saviour narrative, purported to descend from an ancient, hermetic tradition of witchcraft in Italy. A. D. Manns explores this text via an enchanting conjecture: that the writer, medium, and witch Roma Lister played a pivotal role in the formation of both Aradia and, therefore, a new form of paganism called Wicca.

items.title: “That’s Why We Become Witches”: Sylvia Townsend Warner’s Lolly Willowes (1926)
items.summary: A novel about a woman who throws off the yoke of patriarchy to become a witch.

items.title: Hooked on Sonics: Experimenting with Sound in 19th-Century Popular Science
items.summary: Of all the senses cultivated throughout the 19th century, it was the sense of hearing that experienced the most dramatic transformation, as the science of sound underwent rapid advancement. Lucas Thompson delves into a particular genre of popular acoustics primers aimed at children and amateurs alike, which reveal the pedagogical, ludic, and transcendental strivings of Victorian society.

items.title: A Treatise on the All-Healing Qualities of Earth Bathing (1790)
items.summary: A medical tract on the health effects of burying oneself alive in mud.

items.summary: Dioramas avant la lettre that depict local life in Suriname.

items.title: The Nature of the Beast: Charles le Brun’s Human-Animal Hybrids (1806)
items.summary: Illustrations of supposed physiognomic affinities between humans and animals.

items.title: The Adventures and Experiences of the First Slovak Novel
items.summary: Partially banned upon publication and translated into English for the first time this year, *René, or: A Young Man’s Adventures and Experiences* (1783–85) found new readers in the communist era thanks to its critiques of feudalism, capitalism, and the Catholic Church. Dobrota Pucherová introduces us to this hybrid work, which mixes the bildungsroman with the philosophical novel, the romance, the adventure story, the travelogue, the history book, and the orientalist fantasy.

items.title: Vertiginous Accounts: Travels in the Air (1871 edition)
items.summary: Expedition accounts of aeronauts bravely venturing into the heavens on hot-air balloons.

items.title: Birth of the Pearl (1901)
items.summary: A "living picture" film staging Botticelli’s *Birth of Venus* with a twist.

items.title: The End of the World as We (Didn’t) Know It: Now and Then #5
items.summary: A Rapture that wasn’t, in 2025 and 1844.

items.title: The Shadow of Desire: Painting the Origins of Art (ca. 1625–1850)
items.summary: Paintings illustrating Pliny the Elder’s account of the origins of art.

items.title: “You Think Me a Bold Cheat”: Mary Carleton, Counterfeit Princess
items.summary: Accused of posing as foreign royalty to lure her young suitor into a bigamous marriage, Mary Carleton was the subject of dozens of pamphlets and broadsides published in the mid-17th century, including by Carleton herself. Investigating the fraudster’s life, Laura Kolb finds a self-fashioning figure who both influenced the emergence of the English novel and serves as a strange precursor to our modern-day fascination with conwomen and counterfeits, like the heiress manqué Anna Delvey.

items.title: A Collection of Old English Customs, and Curious Bequests and Charities (1842)
items.summary: A compilation of historical gifting traditions in England, with a focus on the peculiar.

items.title: Ivan Aivazovsky’s Miniature Seascapes (ca. 1887)
items.summary: Identical photographs of the artist, each with a unique miniature painting at the centre.

items.title: The Cat’s Maew: Thai Treatise on Auspicious Felines (19th Century)
items.summary: A manuscript that pairs illustrations of cats with poetic descriptions and notes on what mystical benefits their owners might hope to accrue.

items.title: Watching the World in a Dark Room: The Early Modern Camera Obscura
items.summary: Centuries before photography froze the world into neat frames, scientists, poets, and artists streamed transient images into dark interior spaces with the help of a camera obscura. Julie Park explores the early modern fascination with this quasi-spiritual technology and the magic, melancholy, and dream-like experiences it produced.

items.title: Cannibal Modernity: Oswald de Andrade’s Manifesto Antropófago (1928)
items.summary: A modernist manifesto inspired (controversially) by the Tupi people of Brazil.

items.summary: Commemorative manuscript featuring illustrations of pageants, costumes, and fireworks, later further illustrated by a separate artist, with floral motifs.

items.summary: Female astronomical first, in 2025 and 1787.

items.title: Through the Magnifying Glass: The Cheese Mites (1903)

items.title: Splitting Hairs: Chinese Immigrants, the Queue, and the Boundaries of Political Citizenship
items.summary: As Chinese immigration to California accelerated across the 19th century, the hairstyle known as the queue — a long, braided pony tail — became the subject of white Americans’ fascination, disgust, and legal regulation. Sarah Gold McBride explores why hair served as an index of political subjecthood, and how the queue exposed cracks in American norms regarding gender, economy, and citizenship.

items.summary: A crop circle sighting tinged with Christian morality.

items.title: Dancing Naked on the Head of a Pin: The Early History of Microphotography
items.summary: In 1853, John Benjamin Dancer achieved a feat of seemingly impossible scale: he shrunk an image to the size of a sharpened pencil tip. Anika Burgess explores the invention of microphotography and its influence on erotic paraphernalia and military communications.

items.title: Off with Their Heads: Illustrations of Blemmyes (ca. 1175–1724)

items.title: Imagining an Idle Countess: George Wightwick’s The Palace of Architecture
items.summary: In 1840, British architect George Wightwick published a world history of architecture in the Romantic mode, inviting readers to enter a vast garden where Buddhist iconography rubs shoulders with Greek temples and Egyptian pyramids gaze upon Gothic cathedrals. His intended audience? Idle women. Matthew Mullane revisits this visionary but ultimately unpopular text, revealing the legacy of attempts to gatekeep the realms of imagination and fantasy pertaining to the built environment.

items.title: How Flamingos Got Their Pink
items.summary: Against the morphological backdrop of the rest of nature, a giant pink bird on stilts sounds like something out of Lewis Carroll’s imagination. And yet flamingos came out of evolution’s laboratory, surprising and inevitable as the neocortex, so extravagant in their improbability that a group of them is called a flamboyance. But the flamboyance of flamingos does not come from within — it is acquired the way experience and life-history color a person. The story of how pink traveled from volcanos to wings is the story of life on Earth, the beauty of it and the bewilderment of it
